chunks.text: Towards Robust Algorithms against Uncertainty in Learning Systems | EECS at UC Berkeley Towards Robust Algorithms against Uncertainty in Learning Systems Jichan Chung EECS Department, University of California, Berkeley Technical Report No. UCB/EECS-2025-166 August 15, 2025 http://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2025/EECS-2025-166.pdf Recent advances in AI have driven remarkable progress in natural language processing, computer vision, and speech recognition, but the systems that train and deploy these models have grown increasingly large and complex. As AI infrastructures expand â with massive data pipelines, distributed computing, and giant model architectures â they introduce new forms of uncertainty, such as node failures in distributed learning, unpredictable client data in federated learning, and unreliable zero-shot predictions from foundation models. These challenges can undermine the benefits of advanced AI systems, leaving practitioners unsure whether the performance gains outweigh the risks and costs of adoption. To this end, this thesis tackles these challenges by developing training methods that ensure robust learning and reliable gains even in the presence of these uncertainties. First, we introduce ZeroMatch, a framework that combines confidence-based semi-supervised learning with zero-shot pseudo-labels from foundation models, enabling robust learning even with noisy labels. Second, we present clustering methods in federated learning systems, proposing the Iterative Federated Clustering Algorithm (IFCA) and analyzing its convergence under realistic conditions, and U-IFCA which extends IFCA to a more general unsupervised setting, presenting a generative model-based approach that discovers latent client clusters without explicit labels. Finally, we propose robust class parallelism, a redundancy-enhanced variant of class parallelism that mitigates the risks of machine

(IFCA) and analyzing its convergence under realistic conditions, and U-IFCA which extends IFCA to a more general unsupervised setting, presenting a generative model-based approach that discovers latent client clusters without explicit labels. Finally, we propose robust class parallelism, a redundancy-enhanced variant of class parallelism that mitigates the risks of machine unavailability in distributed training. Together, these contributions offer practical methods to make large-scale AI systems more reliable in uncertain conditions.
